blocks.body: Verbs + Gerund brings together common verbs that are naturally followed by the -ing form.

{"level":"B1","intro":"In this lesson you collect common verbs that need a gerund after them and start to treat these combinations as ready-made patterns, not isolated words.","rules":[{"label":"Ready-made pattern","color":"emerald","text":"With many verbs, it is best to remember verb + -ing as a whole: enjoy reading, avoid making.","example":"She enjoys reading."},{"label":"Often no infinitive","color":"blue","text":"After many of these verbs, the to-infinitive either sounds odd or is simply a grammar mistake.","example":"suggest going, not suggest to go"},{"label":"Group the verbs","color":"amber","text":"It is easier to learn these verbs by meaning: liking, avoiding, continuing, suggesting, considering.","example":"mind waiting / keep working / consider moving"}]}

{"title":"1. Common patterns","intro":"Here are a few useful groups of verbs that usually take a gerund.","items":[{"label":"Liking / reaction","title":"enjoy / mind / dislike + -ing","subtitle":"enjoy reading / mind waiting / dislike waking up early"},{"label":"Avoiding / considering","title":"avoid / consider + -ing","subtitle":"avoid making mistakes / consider moving abroad"},{"label":"Suggestion / recommendation","title":"suggest / recommend + -ing","subtitle":"suggest taking a break / recommend booking now"},{"label":"Continuation / completion","title":"keep / finish + -ing","subtitle":"keep working / finish writing"}]}

{"title":"2. How these patterns work","sections":[{"label":"Liking and habits","color":"emerald","description":"Verbs like enjoy, mind, dislike often show an attitude to an action as an activity or process.","examples":[{"en":"I enjoy cooking for friends.","ua":"I enjoy cooking for friends."},{"en":"Do you mind waiting outside?","ua":"Do you mind waiting outside?"},{"en":"She dislikes working late.","ua":"She dislikes working late."}]},{"label":"Avoiding, suggesting, considering","color":"blue","description":"Verbs such as avoid, suggest, recommend, consider often present the action as an option, a solution, or something to avoid.","examples":[{"en":"Try to avoid using your phone while driving.","ua":"Try to avoid using your phone while driving."},{"en":"He suggested meeting after lunch.","ua":"He suggested meeting after lunch."},{"en":"We are considering renting a smaller office.","ua":"We are considering renting a smaller office."}],"note":"After suggest and recommend, the gerund sounds especially natural in neutral English."},{"label":"Continuing or finishing an action","color":"amber","description":"Verbs like keep, continue, finish focus on the duration or completion of a process.","examples":[{"en":"Please keep talking.","ua":"Please keep talking."},{"en":"She finished writing the email.","ua":"She finished writing the email."},{"en":"They continued working despite the noise.","ua":"They continued working despite the noise."}],"note":"Here the gerund highlights the action as a process."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using a to-infinitive after suggest.","wrong":"She suggested to leave earlier.","right":"✅ She suggested leaving earlier.","hint":"In the basic pattern after suggest, the gerund sounds natural."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using the infinitive after mind.","wrong":"Do you mind to wait a minute?","right":"✅ Do you mind waiting a minute?","hint":"After mind, the standard choice is the -ing form."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Treating avoid like a verb that takes an infinitive.","wrong":"He avoided to answer the question.","right":"✅ He avoided answering the question.","hint":"After avoid, use a gerund because it names the action the person avoids."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaway","items":["After many verbs, the natural pattern is verb + gerund.","The gerund appears especially often after enjoy, mind, avoid, suggest, recommend, consider, keep, finish.","After suggest and recommend, do not rush to add a to-infinitive automatically.","If the verb describes attitude to an action, avoidance, suggestion, or the continuation of a process, the gerund is often the safe choice.","Learn such verbs together with a short example, not as isolated words."]}
